description: Blood test results from Rythm Health. Each key biomarker gets its own panel with Rythm's 'Performance Range' (green = optimal). Import new results via scripts/import_blood_test.py.

panels.description: AI-generated wellness summary updated each fetch cycle. Requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in env.

options.defaultContent:
Waiting for AI summary. Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and wait for a fetch cycle, or click Regenerate.

↻ Regenerate now

targets.query: SELECT last("summary") AS "summary" FROM "AIHealthSummary" WHERE "dashboard" = 'blood_work'

panels.title: AI Wellness Summary

panels.description: ApoB counts every artery-clogging (atherogenic) lipoprotein particle — one ApoB molecule per LDL, VLDL and remnant particle — making it the single best predictor of heart-attack risk, more reliable than LDL-C. For a 35-year-old man focused on long-term prevention, aim for 80 mg/dL or below (optimal 50-80); 90+ is the conventional 'high' cutoff. Lower is better, with no level too low.

targets.query: SELECT "apob" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: ApoB

panels.description: LDL ('bad' cholesterol) is the cholesterol carried in artery-clogging particles; it drives plaque buildup over decades. Standard lab range is 40-150 mg/dL, but for a 35-year-old man playing the long game, under ~100 is preferable and under 70 is ideal for aggressive prevention. Cross-check with ApoB — ApoB is the more reliable risk number.

targets.query: SELECT "ldl" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: LDL Cholesterol

panels.description: HDL ('good' cholesterol) helps clear cholesterol out of artery walls, so higher is generally protective. For a man, 40 mg/dL is the minimum and the optimal window is 56-100. Cardio exercise, healthy fats and not smoking raise it. Very high HDL (>100) is not extra protective and can occasionally signal other issues.

targets.query: SELECT "hdl" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: HDL Cholesterol

panels.description: Triglycerides are the main fat circulating in blood; high levels track with excess carbs/alcohol, insulin resistance and metabolic risk. Standard 'normal' is under 150 mg/dL, but the optimal window for a healthy 35-year-old man is 35-90. Best measured fasting — a recent meal inflates the number.

targets.query: SELECT "triglycerides" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Triglycerides

panels.description: Total cholesterol sums HDL + LDL + a fraction of triglycerides — a blunt number on its own. Context matters: a high total driven by high HDL is benign, while a high total driven by LDL is the real concern. For a 35-year-old man, lean on ApoB and the LDL/HDL split rather than this figure alone. Standard range 100-240 mg/dL.

targets.query: SELECT "total_cholesterol" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Total Cholesterol

panels.description: Remnant cholesterol is the cholesterol in triglyceride-rich 'leftover' particles (VLDL/IDL), increasingly recognized as highly artery-clogging independent of LDL. Calculated as total minus HDL minus LDL. For a 35-year-old man, keep it under 20 mg/dL; rising values usually track with triglycerides and insulin resistance.

targets.query: SELECT "remnant_cholesterol" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Remnant Cholesterol

panels.description: This ratio (total cholesterol divided by HDL) is a quick cardiovascular-risk snapshot — it improves when HDL rises or LDL falls. For a man, under 3.5 is good and under 3 is optimal; 5 or above signals elevated risk. Lower is better.

targets.query: SELECT "total_chol_hdl_ratio" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Total Chol/HDL Ratio

panels.description: One of the best low-cost proxies for insulin resistance — a high ratio means lots of small dense LDL and poorer metabolic health. For a 35-year-old man, under 1.5 is good and under 1.25 is optimal; above 2-3 suggests insulin resistance worth addressing with diet and exercise. Lower is better.

targets.query: SELECT "trig_hdl_ratio" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Triglycerides/HDL Ratio

panels.description: This ratio estimates average LDL particle size. A higher ratio means larger, fluffier, less dangerous particles; a lower ratio means small dense LDL that penetrates artery walls more easily. For a 35-year-old man, 1.35-1.65 is the favorable zone.

targets.query: SELECT "ldl_apob_ratio" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: LDL/ApoB Ratio

panels.description: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein measures low-grade, body-wide inflammation — a key amplifier of cardiovascular risk. AHA bands: under 1 mg/L low risk, 1-3 moderate, over 3 high. For a healthy 35-year-old man, aim under 1. It spikes transiently with infections, injuries or hard workouts in the days before a draw, so re-test before reading much into a single high value.

targets.query: SELECT "hscrp" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: hs-CRP (Cardiovascular Inflammation)

panels.description: Vitamin D (25-hydroxy) supports immune function, bone health, mood and testosterone production. Standard sufficiency is 30+ ng/mL, but the optimal window for a 35-year-old man is 55-80. Most people in northern climates run low through winter; supplementation usually closes the gap. Above ~100 risks toxicity.

targets.query: SELECT "vitamin_d" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Vitamin D

panels.description: Ferritin reflects the body's iron stores. For an adult man, 30+ ng/mL avoids deficiency and the optimal window is 100-400. Low ferritin causes fatigue and poor endurance; high ferritin can mean iron overload (hemochromatosis is more common in men) or simply inflammation, since ferritin also rises as an inflammatory marker — cross-check with hs-CRP.

targets.query: SELECT "ferritin" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Ferritin

panels.description: Creatinine is a muscle-breakdown waste product the kidneys filter out, making it a standard kidney-function marker. For a 35-year-old man, 0.8-1.1 mg/dL is optimal and up to ~1.2 is normal. Mildly high readings are common in muscular men or after a hard workout or dehydration — an eGFR calculation gives a fuller picture of kidney function.

targets.query: SELECT "creatinine" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Creatinine

panels.description: Albumin is the most abundant blood protein, made by the liver; it reflects liver synthesis, protein nutrition and hydration. For a 35-year-old man, 4-5 g/dL is optimal. Low albumin can signal liver or kidney issues or poor protein intake; a slightly high value usually just means dehydration.

targets.query: SELECT "albumin" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Albumin

panels.description: Total testosterone is the sum of bound and free testosterone — the primary male sex hormone, driving muscle, energy, mood, libido and recovery. The standard adult-male lab range is 200-800 ng/dL; the athletic-optimal target here is 700-1100. For a 35-year-old man, readings in the 200s-400s, though technically 'normal', sit low and are worth discussing with a doctor alongside any symptoms.

targets.query: SELECT "total_testosterone" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Total Testosterone

panels.description: Free testosterone is the small unbound fraction that is actually biologically active — often more informative than total T, especially when SHBG is abnormal. Lab range 46-224 pg/mL; optimal target 120-180. For a 35-year-old man, a free-T below ~120 alongside symptoms like low energy, low libido or poor recovery is worth a clinical conversation.

targets.query: SELECT "free_testosterone" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Free Testosterone

panels.description: Estrogen (estradiol) — men need a moderate amount — supports bone density, libido and mood; the male optimal is 20-30 pg/mL, with both too-low and too-high causing problems. SHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin) is the protein that binds testosterone and estrogen, so higher SHBG means less free hormone available. For a 35-year-old man, SHBG of 30-50 nmol/L is the target — high SHBG can drag free testosterone down even when total T looks fine.

targets.query: SELECT "estrogen" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC
SELECT "shbg" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Estrogen & SHBG

panels.description: TSH is the pituitary's signal to the thyroid — it rises when the thyroid is underactive and falls when overactive, so it reads inversely to thyroid output. The lab range is wide (0.45-4.5 uIU/mL); optimal for a 35-year-old man is 0.5-2.5. A TSH creeping above ~2.5-3 alongside fatigue or weight changes is worth re-checking with Free T3/T4.

targets.query: SELECT "tsh" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: TSH

panels.description: Free T3 is the active thyroid hormone that actually sets metabolic rate, body temperature and energy — the most functional thyroid marker. For a 35-year-old man, 3.4-4.4 pg/mL is optimal; a low Free T3 despite a normal TSH can explain fatigue, cold intolerance and a sluggish metabolism.

targets.query: SELECT "free_t3" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Free T3

panels.description: Fructosamine measures the glycation (sugar-coating) of blood proteins, reflecting average glucose over the past 2-3 weeks — a shorter-window companion to HbA1c. For a 35-year-old man, 210-260 umol/L is optimal (lab reference 205-285); rising values mean recent average blood sugar is climbing, an early metabolic-health flag worth catching before it becomes prediabetes.

targets.query: SELECT "fructosamine" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Fructosamine (2-3 week glucose)

panels.description: Vitamin B12 is essential for nerve function, red blood cell production and energy metabolism; the body can't make it, so it comes from animal foods or supplements. For a 35-year-old man, 400-900 pg/mL is optimal — well above the 232 deficiency threshold. Low B12 causes fatigue, tingling and brain fog, and is common in vegetarians/vegans and long-term antacid users.

targets.query: SELECT "b12" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Vitamin B12

panels.description: Uric acid is the waste product of purine breakdown; high levels can crystallize in joints (gout) and are linked to high blood pressure and metabolic risk. For a 35-year-old man, 4-6 mg/dL is optimal and up to ~7 is the conventional ceiling. High readings track with red meat, alcohol, fructose and excess weight; lower is generally better.

targets.query: SELECT "uric_acid" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Uric Acid

panels.description: GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) is a liver enzyme and a sensitive early marker of liver stress, bile-duct problems and alcohol load — it's also an independent oxidative-stress and cardiovascular risk signal. For a 35-year-old man, 10-40 U/L is optimal; values rising toward the 71 ceiling warrant a look at alcohol intake and metabolic health. Lower is better.

targets.query: SELECT "ggt" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: GGT (liver enzyme)

panels.description: ALP is an enzyme produced mainly by the liver and bones. In a healthy adult man it mostly reflects liver and bile-duct health; abnormal levels can flag obstructed bile flow or unusual bone turnover. For a 35-year-old man, 50-100 U/L is the optimal window, with 40-129 the broad normal range. Low ALP is uncommon and can hint at a zinc or nutritional deficiency.

targets.query: SELECT "alkaline_phosphatase" FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time ASC

panels.title: Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP)

panels.description: All biomarkers from the most recent blood test.

targets.query: SELECT * FROM "BloodTest" WHERE $timeFilter ORDER BY time DESC LIMIT 1

panels.title: Latest blood test results

title: Blood Work
